content.text: From Boltzmann Machine to RBM

A full Boltzmann machine connects every unit to every other unit. That generality makes inference intractable: computing the conditional probability of any single unit requires summing over all 2^(n−1) configurations of the remaining units. The Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) breaks this deadlock by removing all within-layer connections, keeping only between-layer connections.

attrs.alt: Left: full Boltzmann machine with within-layer edges (intractable). Right: RBM bipartite structure — hidden and visible layers with between-layer edges only

content.text: The RBM Energy Function
An RBM with visible units v ∈ {0,1}ᵈ and hidden units h ∈ {0,1}ᵏ has the joint energy:
b ∈ Rᵈ are visible biases, c ∈ Rᵏ are hidden biases, and W ∈ Rᵈˣᵏ is the weight matrix connecting visible unit i to hidden unit j. No Wᵥᵥ or Wₕₕ terms exist — this is the restriction.
Tractable Conditional Distributions
Because there are no within-layer edges, all hidden units are conditionally independent given the visible units, and vice versa:

where σ(x) = 1/(1+e⁻ˣ) is the sigmoid function. Both are single forward passes through a linear layer followed by sigmoid — fully tractable. This is what makes block Gibbs sampling feasible: sample all hidden units in parallel given v, then sample all visible units in parallel given h.
Contrastive Divergence Training
The log-likelihood gradient for the RBM weight W_{ij} is:

attrs.latex: \frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}

content.text: The data term ⟨vᵢhⱼ⟩_data = vᵢ P(hⱼ=1|v) is easy. The model term ⟨vᵢhⱼ⟩_model requires sampling from P(v,h) — expensive. Contrastive Divergence-k (CD-k) approximates the model term with k steps of Gibbs sampling starting from the data:
Start from a training example v⁰.
Sample h⁰ ~ P(h|v⁰).
Sample v¹ ~ P(v|h⁰).
Sample h¹ ~ P(h|v¹).
Use ⟨v⁰ᵢ h⁰ⱼ⟩ − ⟨v¹ᵢ h¹ⱼ⟩ as the weight gradient (CD-1).
Training on Binary MNIST

# Minimal training loop on binarised MNIST
# pip install tensorflow  (for dataset loading only)
import numpy as np
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import mnist

(X_train, _), (X_test, _) = mnist.load_data()
# Binarise and flatten
X_train = (X_train.reshape(-1, 784) / 255.0 > 0.5).astype(float)
X_test  = (X_test.reshape(-1, 784)  / 255.0 > 0.5).astype(float)

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
rbm = RBM(n_visible=784, n_hidden=256, rng=rng)

batch_size = 64
for epoch in range(10):
    perm = rng.permutation(len(X_train))
    for start in range(0, len(X_train), batch_size):
        batch = X_train[perm[start:start+batch_size]]
        rbm.cd1_update(batch, lr=0.01)
    recon_err = rbm.reconstruction_error(X_test[:1000])
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}:  reconstruction MSE = {recon_err:.4f}")
# Epoch 1:  reconstruction MSE ≈ 0.044
# Epoch 10: reconstruction MSE ≈ 0.021
Generating New Samples
To generate a new sample, run block Gibbs sampling for many steps starting from a random visible state. In practice, a few hundred steps is usually sufficient to produce visually coherent samples:
RBMs as Feature Extractors

After training, P(h|v) maps each visible input to a distribution over hidden unit activations. These activations serve as learned features — each hidden unit detects a pattern in the visible layer that co-occurs with a learned subset of visible units. Hinton's pre-training work (2006) used stacked RBMs to initialise deep belief networks, which was the first demonstration that deep networks could be trained reliably. This is now largely superseded by initialisation schemes like He/Xavier init and batch normalisation, but the representational insight holds: RBMs learn to encode the statistical structure of their training data in the hidden layer.
